advantages: Provides a standardized, reproducible rule for when sample evidence contradicts a default claim.
Explicitly controls Type I error rate via pre-specified α.
Works across domains—means, proportions, variances—with the same H₀/H₁/p-value logic.
Pairs naturally with confidence intervals for equivalent two-sided decisions while CIs add effect-size context.
Scales to experimental design: power analysis helps choose sample size before running costly LLM evaluations.
disadvantages: Binary reject/fail-to-reject oversimplifies continuous uncertainty; p-values near α are ambiguous.
Misinterpretation is common (p-value as P(H₀ true), "non-significant" as proof of no effect).
Sensitive to sample size: with huge n, trivial effects become "significant" without practical importance.
Assumptions (independence, approximate Normality, equal variance) may be violated in messy production logs.
Multiple comparisons without correction inflate Type I errors when many models or metrics are tested.
